behaviors.description: Speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure could exist at the code level (not threading speaker labels through) OR at the STT API level (not returning diarization). REAL-INFRA: Real HTTP to the STT API is needed. MOCK-HIDE: A stub STT response hard-codes whatever speaker labels the test author invents — it cannot reveal whether the real STT API returns diarization data at all, whether it handles overlapping speakers, or whether its label format matches what the downstream parser expects. LLM-DEP: Speaker diarization is an ML/AI task; the real model's ability to identify and consistently label speakers across a real audio stream is non-deterministic and cannot be validated by a stub. REJECTION-GATE: Nothing in the behavior description limits the test to 'does the parser correctly handle a label field that is already present' — the behavior explicitly couples STT output quality to summary attribution, so we cannot demote below Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real audio recording with at least two identifiable speakers to the live STT API. Assert that the response payload contains speaker labels (e.g., 'Speaker 1', 'Speaker 2') and that each transcript segment is tagged with a speaker identifier. Then verify that downstream parsing retains those labels in the structure passed to the LLM prompt.

behaviors.description: The structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-HIDE: A stub LLM returns whatever action items the test author pre-programs. It cannot reveal whether a real model fails to extract an assignment buried in indirect phrasing ('let's have Sarah handle that'), misattributes it, or omits one of several assigned attendees. LLM-DEP: Correctness depends entirely on the LLM's ability to identify task assignments and link them to specific attendees — a classification/extraction judgment made by the real model. Deterministic-assertion trap: the assertion 'at least one action item per assigned attendee' is perfectly deterministic, but the fixture that validates it replaces the LLM's actual judgment with a constant, making any extraction failure invisible. REJECTION-GATE: No part of the behavior constrains the test to infrastructure (routing, retries, payload shape) independent of model output — the whole behavior is about what the model produces.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a real curated transcript (with named attendees and explicit task assignments) to the live LLM summarization step. Assert that the returned 'action_items' list contains at least one entry per attendee who was explicitly assigned a task, with the correct attendee name attached. Run against multiple transcript samples covering direct ('Bob will do X') and indirect ('let's have Carol handle Y') phrasing.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in the narrative.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: The failure is the LLM misclassifying a decision as narrative — a content-routing judgment that only the real model makes. A stub always returns the correct bucket because the test author writes what goes where, making every stub test a tautology. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the LLM replaces its classification logic with a constant; any prompt-engineering failure or model regression that causes decisions to leak into the narrative section is completely invisible. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior is not about whether the code builds a 'decisions' key in the JSON (that is a schema test, Unit/Integration) — it is about whether the real LLM routes content into the right section. Nothing in the description limits it to schema shape alone.
behaviors.test_description: Send a known transcript containing clearly stated decisions (e.g., 'We decided to launch in Q3') and deliberative narrative to the live LLM. Assert that every pre-identified decision phrase appears in the 'decisions' list and that none appear exclusively in a 'summary' or 'narrative' field. Use multiple transcripts with decisions stated at varying explicitness levels.

behaviors.description: When the audio file is longer than 60 minutes, the service splits it into segments and processes them sequentially, merging the results into a single coherent summary.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The behavior explicitly chains: (a) audio split logic, (b) STT call per segment (multiple real ML calls — speaker diarization is non-deterministic across segment boundaries), (c) LLM merge call that must produce a coherent unified output. These are multiple real agentic steps in sequence. MOCK-HIDE: Stubbing the STT per segment hides whether speaker labels remain consistent across split boundaries (a real diarization model may re-label 'Speaker 1' differently in each chunk). Stubbing the LLM merge hides whether it actually produces coherent output when stitching potentially discontinuous segment summaries. LLM-DEP: 'Single coherent summary' is a quality judgment on the merge step — only a real LLM call can fail this. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior names both sequential processing of segments AND a coherent merged result; neither the sequential orchestration nor the merge quality can be validated by stubs without hiding real failures.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real audio recording exceeding 60 minutes to the full service. Assert that: (1) the service produces multiple intermediate segment transcripts rather than one monolithic call, (2) speaker labels are consistent or reconciled across segment boundaries, (3) the final merged summary is a single coherent document with no duplicate action items or decisions from the segment boundary, and (4) the 'decisions' and 'action_items' fields represent the whole meeting, not just one segment.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output — completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The behavior explicitly names four sequential pipeline stages, each crossing a real boundary: (1) upload (real network/storage), (2) transcribe (real STT AI/ML API), (3) summarize (real LLM), (4) deliver structured output (real egress). No stage can be stubbed without hiding failures at that boundary. REAL-INFRA: Real HTTP, real file storage, and real external APIs are all required — in-process fakes collapse the boundary conditions where real failures occur (timeouts, auth errors, format mismatches). LLM-DEP: Both STT and LLM steps are real AI/ML calls; any stub replaces non-deterministic model behavior with a constant. REJECTION-GATE: 'Real meeting recording' and 'without manual intervention' are explicit disqualifiers for any lower level — the behavior is definitionally an end-to-end smoke test of the full agentic pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Using a real meeting audio file (15–30 minutes, multiple speakers, with known decisions and task assignments), run the full service pipeline end-to-end: upload the file, trigger transcription, trigger summarization, and retrieve the structured output. Assert that the pipeline completes autonomously, the output is valid JSON matching the schema, contains non-empty 'decisions' and 'action_items' arrays, and that at least one attendee from the known recording is named. Measure and assert total wall-clock time is within an acceptable SLA.
